blocks.text: A program that always does the same thing isn't very interesting. Conditionals let your code take different paths based on what's happening right now. This is how games respond to what you do, how apps show different things to different users, how anything interactive works.

blocks.alt: Diagram showing the flow of an if/else statement.
blocks.caption: Conditionals let your code take different paths based on conditions.

lines: // runs when the first was false but this is true
// runs when nothing above was true

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: When you click a square, the game asks: is this the right cell? If yes — register the click. If the player has clicked enough — check the result. If wrong — end the game. Every one of those decisions is an if/else.

When you have more than two outcomes, chain `else if` blocks between the opening `if` and the final `else`. JavaScript reads them top to bottom and runs the first one whose condition is true — then skips the rest.

blocks.alt: Diagram showing the flow of an if/else statement.
blocks.caption: Conditionals let your code take different paths based on conditions.

lines: console.log('Good job! You got a B.');
// score is 75, so the second condition is true
// JavaScript checks top to bottom and stops at the first true one

blocks.label: Top to bottom — first match wins
blocks.text: JavaScript checks each condition in order. The moment it finds a true one, it runs that block and skips the rest. If you put a less specific condition first, a more specific one below it might never run.

exercises.title: Which branch runs?
tasks: score is 75. Which message does this code print?
exercises.hint: Go through each condition from top to bottom. Stop at the first one that's true.
exercises.explanation: 75 >= 90 is false. 75 >= 80 is false. 75 >= 70 is true — so the third branch runs and prints 'Grade: C'. JavaScript skips the else entirely.

exercises.title: First match wins
tasks: score is 100. What does this code print?
exercises.hint: JavaScript checks conditions top to bottom and stops the moment it finds a true one.
exercises.explanation: score >= 50 is true for 100, so 'You passed!' prints immediately. JavaScript skips the else if — it never checks score >= 90. First match wins, always.

exercises.title: Sort scores into grades
tasks: This chain prints 'A' when score >= 90, 'B' when score >= 80, 'C' when score >= 70, and 'F' otherwise.
Drag each score value into the bucket showing what it will print.
exercises.hint: Run each value through the conditions top to bottom. Stop at the first true one. Watch out — >= includes the exact boundary number.
exercises.explanation: score = 90 prints 'A' — >= 90 is true for exactly 90, not just above it. score = 70 prints 'C' — the first two conditions are false, but score >= 70 is true.

exercises.title: Complete the if/else
tasks: Drop the correct keywords into the blanks to complete this Simon Says cell check.
exercises.hint: The first keyword starts the check and needs a condition in parentheses. The second keyword is the fallback with no condition.
codeWithBlanks: console.log('Correct! Simon says you got it.');
exercises.explanation: `if` opens the check and requires a condition in parentheses. `else` is the fallback — it has no condition and runs when everything above it was false.

exercises.title: Complete the comparison
tasks: Drop the correct tokens to complete the click check. The condition should compare playerClicks to targetClicks.
exercises.hint: In JavaScript, === compares values without side effects. A single = would assign instead of compare.
exercises.explanation: `===` compares values strictly. `==` does type coercion and can cause surprises. A single `=` assigns — it doesn't compare — which is one of the most common JavaScript bugs inside conditions.

exercises.title: Put the levels in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order to print the right Simon Says level message for level = 3.
exercises.hint: The variable must be declared first. else always comes last and has no condition.
exercises.explanation: `else` must be last — it has no condition and acts as the catch-all. The more specific condition (>= 5) goes first so level = 5 doesn't get caught by >= 3.

exercises.title: Type the condition
tasks: Fill in the blank: the condition should check whether lives equals 0.
exercises.hint: Use === to compare — not = which assigns. The variable is called lives.
exercises.explanation: `lives === 0` is the comparison. Writing `lives = 0` inside the condition would assign zero to lives and always be truthy — the else would never run. This is why === matters.

exercises.title: Trace the Simon Says check
tasks: cellClicked is 3, correctCell is 5. What does this code print?
exercises.hint: Evaluate each condition in order. Which one is the first to be true?
exercises.explanation: 3 === 5 is false. 3 < 5 is true — so 'Too early.' prints. The else block never runs because the second branch matched first.

exercises.title: Add the else if branch
tasks: Fill in the blanks for the middle branch: it should run when score is at least 50.
exercises.hint: The keyword is two words: else if. The condition compares score to 50.
exercises.explanation: `else if` adds a second check that only runs when the first was false. The condition goes in parentheses right after `else if`. For score = 65: >= 90 is false, so the else if fires.

exercises.title: Write your first if/else
tasks: lives is 2. Write an if/else that prints 'Still alive!' when lives > 0, and 'Game over.' otherwise.
Hit Run with lives = 2, then change it to 0 and run again to confirm both branches work.
exercises.hint: if (lives > 0) { ... } else { ... }. The condition goes in parentheses after if.
exercises.starterCode:
let lives = 2;

// Write your if/else here

steps.text: Hit Run — the starter code just declares lives. Nothing prints yet.
Write: if (lives > 0) { console.log('Still alive!'); }. Hit Run.
Add the else block: } else { console.log('Game over.'); }. Hit Run.
Change lives to 0 on line 1 and run again — you should see 'Game over.'
Both branches work — that's a complete if/else!

exercises.title: Build the grade chain
tasks: score is 82. Write an if/else if/else chain:
- 'Grade: A' when score >= 90
- 'Grade: B' when score >= 80
- 'Grade: C' when score >= 70
- 'Grade: F' otherwise
Hit Run — you should see 'Grade: B'. Then test with score = 65.
exercises.hint: Start with if (score >= 90). Then else if for 80 and 70. End with else.
exercises.starterCode:
let score = 82;

// Your if/else if/else chain here

steps.text: Add: if (score >= 90) { console.log('Grade: A'); }. Hit Run — nothing prints yet since 82 < 90.
Add: else if (score >= 80) { console.log('Grade: B'); }. Hit Run — you should see 'Grade: B'.
Add: else if (score >= 70) { console.log('Grade: C'); } else { console.log('Grade: F'); }. Hit Run.
Change score to 65 on line 1. Hit Run — you should now see 'Grade: F'.
Full if/else if/else chain working! This exact pattern decides Simon Says difficulty levels.

exercises.title: Simon Says click handler
tasks: Write the decision logic Simon Says uses when a player clicks a cell.
Three branches:
1. if cellClicked === correctCell → log 'Correct! Click registered.'
2. else if clickCount === targetClicks → log 'Round complete! Well done.'
3. else → log 'Wrong cell. Game over.'
exercises.hint: Use === for every comparison. Three separate outcomes need if, else if, and else.

steps.text: Hit Run to see the starter variables. Nothing prints yet.
Add: if (cellClicked === correctCell) { console.log('Correct! Click registered.'); }. Hit Run.
Add: else if (clickCount === targetClicks) { console.log('Round complete! Well done.'); }. Hit Run.
Add: else { console.log('Wrong cell. Game over.'); }. Hit Run.

checks.label: still logs 'Correct!' since cellClicked still matches

steps.text: Change cellClicked to 5 on line 1. Hit Run — you should see 'Wrong cell. Game over.'
That's exactly how Simon Says handles every click — three branches, three outcomes.

exercises.title: Combine conditions with &&
tasks: playerLevel is 5 and hasKey is true.
Write an if that logs 'You can enter the castle!' only when playerLevel >= 5 AND hasKey is true.
Write a second if that logs 'Game over. Please restart.' only when bossDefeated is false AND playerLevel < 3.
Test by changing playerLevel to 2 and running again.
exercises.hint: Combine two conditions with &&: if (playerLevel >= 5 && hasKey === true). Both must be true for the block to run.

steps.text: Hit Run — nothing prints yet. Read the three starter variables.
Write the castle check: if (playerLevel >= 5 && hasKey === true) → log 'You can enter the castle!'. Hit Run.

checks.label: hasKey is part of the condition
'You can enter the castle!' is logged

steps.text: Write the game over check: if (bossDefeated === false && playerLevel < 3) → log 'Game over. Please restart.'. Hit Run.
Change playerLevel to 2 on line 1 and run. Castle message disappears (level < 5). Game over message appears (level < 3 and boss not defeated).
&& means BOTH conditions must be true. That's how Simon Says checks multiple requirements at once.

challenges.title: The else block never runs
challenges.description: No matter what score is set to, only the first message prints. Why?
challenges.hint: Think about the first condition. Is score >= 0 true for any score?
challenges.explanation: score >= 0 is true for every possible score (including 40), so the very first branch always wins. The else if and else never get a chance to run. Fix: reorder so the most specific conditions come first — check >= 50 before >= 0.

challenges.title: The discount code only works sometimes
challenges.description: The store should give a discount when the customer has a coupon OR has spent more than $100. But it's only giving the discount when BOTH are true.
challenges.hint: Should both conditions need to be true, or just one?

checks.name: getsDiscount
checks.label: getsDiscount is true when hasCoupon is true

challenges.explanation: && requires both conditions. The customer has a coupon but only spent $50. Since amountSpent > 100 is false, the whole && expression is false. Change && to || so having either condition is enough.

challenges.title: The game never ends
challenges.description: The game should end when lives reach 0 OR score hits 100. Find the three bugs.
challenges.hint: Count the equals signs in the condition. What do single, double, and triple equals each do?
challenges.explanation: There are three = signs where there should be === (comparison, not assignment). lives = 0 SETS lives to 0 inside the condition — it doesn't check if lives IS 0. Fix all three: lives === 0 || score === 100.
